text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
ROUTINE ONCOLOGIC SURVEILLANCE

CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old female with history of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head/uncinate process, treated with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX followed by long-course capecitabine-based chemoradiation and subsequently pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with negative margins (R0; pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB). Completed adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (6 cycles). Now presenting for scheduled 12-month post-adjuvant surveillance imaging. Rising serum CA 19-9 (67 U/mL; prior value 19 U/mL approximately 6 months ago) and CEA (4.8 ng/mL; prior 2.2 ng/mL). Evaluate for disease recurrence.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed on a 128-slice scanner following administration of 100 mL of iohexol (Omnipaque 350) intravenous contrast. Images were acquired in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis (60-second delay). Non-contrast and arterial phase images were additionally acquired through the abdomen for lesion characterization. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats were reviewed in addition to axial source images. CT chest was performed in standard fashion without dedicated arterial phase. Slice thickness: 2.5 mm axial throughout; 5 mm coronal and sagittal reformats.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast approximately 6 months prior. CT abdomen/pelvis approximately 3 months prior (3-month surveillance scan).

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: Two new pulmonary nodules are identified that were definitively absent on the prior comparison studies. A well-circumscribed, non-calcified, solid nodule measuring 7 mm in greatest dimension is seen in the right lower lobe (series 3, image 44), situated in the posterior basal segment, with smooth margins. A second non-calcified solid nodule measuring 5 mm is present in the left lower lobe (series 3, image 51), posterior basal segment, also smooth and solid in morphology. Neither nodule demonstrates associated ground-glass change, spiculation, or pleural tethering. No cavitation. No endobronchial lesion. No hilar or mediastinal mass. The central airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. No consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. No pericardial effusion.

Mediastinum: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The heart is normal in size. Aorta and great vessels are within normal limits for caliber. No anterior mediastinal mass.

Pleura: No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pleural thickening or nodularity to suggest pleural seeding.

Bony Thorax: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine, appropriate for age.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: There is a new, well-defined hypodense lesion in the right lobe of the liver, hepatic segment VI, measuring approximately 1.2 cm in greatest axial dimension (series 6, image 78). On the non-contrast series, this lesion is hypodense relative to background liver parenchyma. On arterial phase imaging, faint peripheral rim-like enhancement is noted. On portal venous phase, the lesion demonstrates relative washout in comparison to the enhancing hepatic parenchyma, appearing hypodense. The morphology and enhancement characteristics are atypical for a simple hepatic cyst (no imperceptible wall, no water density on non-contrast images) and atypical for a hemangioma (no nodular peripheral fill-in). No satellite lesions are identified around this nodule. The remainder of the hepatic parenchyma appears homogeneous without additional focal lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation. The hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis appears intact without evidence of stricture or biloma. No perihepatic fluid.

Postsurgical Changes and Pancreatic Bed: Expected postoperative anatomy is identified consistent with prior pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy. The pancreaticojejunostomy and gastrojejunostomy anastomoses appear intact. There is stable mild soft tissue density and fibrotic change in the pancreatic bed / surgical resection site, unchanged in appearance compared to prior studies and consistent with expected postoperative fibrosis. No definite local tumor recurrence is identified in the surgical bed, though evaluation in this region remains inherently limited by postoperative distortion and fibrosis. No peripancreatic fluid collection or abscess. The remnant pancreatic body and tail appear atrophic, as expected following partial pancreatectomy, with no focal pancreatic lesion.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodules.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size with maintained corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. Several bilateral tiny subcentimeter hypodense renal cortical lesions, too small to characterize, likely representing simple cysts. No solid renal lesions.

Bowel: The afferent and efferent jejunal limbs associated with the hepaticojejunostomy and gastrojejunostomy appear patent without obstruction. No evidence of internal hernia. No dilated small bowel loops. The visualized large bowel is unremarkable. No pneumatosis or portal venous gas.

Vasculature: The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and superior mesenteric vein are patent without stenosis. No arterial or venous thrombosis identified. Post-Whipple vascular anatomy appears intact.

Free Fluid / Peritoneum: No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or soft tissue implants along the peritoneal surfaces to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The omentum appears normal in attenuation without cake-like thickening or nodularity.

---

PELVIS:

No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic free fluid. Uterus and ovaries are not individually identified, consistent with prior gynecologic history. Bladder appears decompressed but without mural lesion. Bony pelvis without lytic or sclerotic lesion.

---

LYMPH NODES: No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the mediastinal, hilar, axillary, peripancreatic, mesenteric, retroperitoneal, iliac, or inguinal nodal chains. Several subcentimeter retroperitoneal lymph nodes are identified (largest approximately 8 mm short axis), which are unchanged from prior and do not meet morphologic criteria for pathologic enlargement.

BONES: No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions are appreciated throughout the visualized axial and appendicular skeleton. Multilevel mild lumbar spondylosis with endplate changes, appropriate for patient age.

SOFT TISSUES: No soft tissue masses in the abdominal or pelvic wall. No subcutaneous implants.

---

SERUM TUMOR MARKERS (referenced for correlation, as reported from contemporaneous laboratory data):
CA 19-9: 67 U/mL (elevated; upper limit of normal 37 U/mL; previously 19 U/mL approximately 6 months ago — representing a greater than three-fold increase)
CEA: 4.8 ng/mL (elevated above normal; previously 2.2 ng/mL approximately 6 months ago — more than doubling)

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NEW 1.2 CM HYPODENSE LESION, HEPATIC SEGMENT VI: This is a new finding not present on prior surveillance imaging. The enhancement characteristics — arterial phase rim enhancement with portal venous phase relative washout — are atypical for a benign lesion and in the clinical context of a patient with known prior pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and concurrently rising CA 19-9 and CEA, this lesion is highly concerning for hepatic metastasis. Further characterization with dedicated multiphasic liver MRI with a hepatobiliary contrast agent (e.g., gadoxetate disodium/Eovist) is strongly recommended on an urgent basis.

2. TWO NEW SOLID PULMONARY NODULES: A 7 mm right lower lobe nodule and a 5 mm left lower lobe nodule, both new compared to prior imaging. In the oncologic context of a patient with rising serum tumor markers and a new indeterminate hepatic lesion, these nodules are suspicious for early pulmonary metastases, particularly given their bilateral distribution and simultaneous new appearance. Alternative considerations include infectious or inflammatory granulomatous nodules, though this is less favored in aggregate. Correlation with dedicated MRI of the liver is recommended as a priority; chest findings should be followed in that context.

3. NO PERITONEAL IMPLANTS, NO LOCAL RECURRENCE IDENTIFIED: No definite evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis or locoregional recurrence in the surgical bed on this study. Post-surgical fibrotic changes in the pancreatic bed remain stable.

4. STABLE POST-WHIPPLE ANATOMY: Expected postoperative changes including hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy, and gastrojejunostomy are intact and without complication. No anastomotic complication, biloma, or fluid collection.

OVERALL IMPRESSION: The combination of a new indeterminate hepatic lesion, two new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and a contemporaneous significant rise in both CA 19-9 and CEA is highly suspicious for metastatic recurrence of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Urgent further characterization of the hepatic lesion with MRI liver is recommended. Clinical correlation and multidisciplinary oncology review advised.

Radiologist attestation: Images and report reviewed and finalized by attending radiologist.
